feed.title: Wheel of Heaven
feed.subtitle: A long reading of the ancient world's creation traditions through a single working hypothesis: that the beings called Elohim were a small advanced civilization that designed life on Earth. Organized along the precessional cycle, in twelve ages.

entry.title: Book of Enoch (1 Enoch — Book of the Watchers)
entry.content:
1 Enoch, the Book of the Watchers (chapters 1–36) is the most directly WoH-resonant material in the entire pseudepigrapha. The Watchers narrative (chapters 6–11) — the descent of the bnei Elohim, the teaching of forbidden arts, the engendering of the Nephilim — is named in the Raëlian canon's commentary section as one of the texts closer to its truth. Source: Charles 1912 critical edition (Public Domain) as base reference; Knibb 1978 and Nickelsburg 2001 (Hermeneia) serve as scholarly references but are copyrighted and not the ship-text.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: Ge'ez (Charles 1912 critical edition, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: ENO-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 5.

Scope

Initial scope: the Book of the Watchers (chapters 1–36). The other books of 1 Enoch (Parables, Astronomical Book, Dream Visions, Epistle) are deferred.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Exodus
entry.content:
Exodus (Hebrew: Shemot — "Names") picks up where Genesis leaves off: the descendants of Jacob in Egypt, the liberation under Moses, the wilderness journey, the Mount Sinai theophany, the Decalogue, the covenant code, and the construction of the Tabernacle.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: pointed Hebrew Masoretic text (Westminster Leningrad Codex, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: EXO-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 2.

Scope

Initial scope: chapters 1–20 (the liberation arc through the Decalogue). The Tabernacle account (chs 25–31, 35–40) extends the bara / asah / yatsar verb-pattern attention into project-building vocabulary and is the natural extension after the initial scope is signed off.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Ezekiel
entry.content:
Ezekiel opens with one of the most-cited passages in the entire Wheel of Heaven reference corpus — chapter 1's throne-vision, the four chayyot (living creatures), the chashmal (electrum / amber / gleaming metal), the wheels-within-wheels, and the kavod (glory). Chapter 1 will be the project's first non-Genesis WoH Translation, chosen for its strong philological backing (Greenberg's Anchor Bible, Block's NICOT, Zimmerli's Hermeneia), its standing public interest, and its high lens-leverage.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: pointed Hebrew Masoretic text (Westminster Leningrad Codex, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: EZK-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 1 (cross-book pipeline pilot) + Phase 2.

Scope

Initial scope: chapter 1 (the throne-vision; the pipeline pilot). Then chapter 10 (the throne-vision return; the kavod leaves the temple) and chapter 37 (the valley of dry bones — read in Extra-Terrestrials Took Me To Their Planet as the biological-creation machine). Extension to other chapters is a reassessment decision.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Epic of Gilgamesh
entry.content:
The Standard Babylonian Epic of Gilgamesh is the project's first cross-tradition pairing. Tablet XI — the flood narrative — is the single best-attested cross-tradition parallel in all of religious literature, paired with the Genesis 6–9 flood account that the WoH Genesis translation has already engaged. Andrew George's 2003 critical edition (Oxford) is the project's scholarly reference but is copyrighted; the WoH translation works from openly-licensed Akkadian transliterations.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: openly-licensed Akkadian transliterations (ETCSL / CDLI / ORACC).
  • Reference format: GILG-WOH-{tablet-roman}:{line}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 3.

Scope

Initial scope: Tablet XI (the flood narrative). Extension to other tablets is a reassessment decision.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Isaiah
entry.content:
Isaiah is the largest of the prophetic books and one of the most frequently cited in the New Testament. Three scope blocks in the WoH translation queue: the throne-room vision of chapter 6 (parallels Ezekiel 1 and the kavod-tradition vocabulary), the "Little Apocalypse" of chapters 24–27 (cosmic-judgment material), and the suffering-servant text of chapter 53.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: pointed Hebrew Masoretic text (Westminster Leningrad Codex, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: ISA-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 2.

Scope

Initial scope: chapter 6, chapters 24–27, chapter 53. Extension to other chapters is a reassessment decision.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Job
entry.content:
Job opens with the bnei-elohim ("sons of the Elohim") council scene, a load-bearing passage for the WoH divine-council reading. The book closes with the divine speeches of chapters 38–42, containing the behemoth and leviathan material that is one of the most lens-charged sections of the Hebrew Bible. The Raëlian canon also notes the Dhorme/RNKJV translation divergence at Job 37–38 explicitly — this translation will surface it.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: pointed Hebrew Masoretic text (Westminster Leningrad Codex, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: JOB-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 2.

Scope

Initial scope: chapters 1–2 (the divine-council frame), chapters 38–42 (the divine speeches with behemoth and leviathan). The wisdom dialogue (chs 3–37) is deferred.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Matthew
entry.content:
Matthew is the most heavily cited New Testament book in the Raëlian canon. The Parable of the Sower in chapter 13 is the canon's most explicit cosmological reading from the Gospels — read as a description of life being seeded on multiple worlds. The translation will be from the polytonic Greek (Tregelles edition), the oldest Public Domain critical edition of the Greek NT.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: polytonic Greek New Testament (Tregelles 1857–1879, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: MAT-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 4.

Scope

Initial scope: complete Gospel. Substantial commentary at the messianic passages (chs 1–2, 16–17, 24–26), the Sermon on the Mount (chs 5–7), and the parables of chapter 13.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Qur'an (selected suras)
entry.content:
Selected suras of the Qur'an. Scope strictly limited to Suras 21:1–5, 54:1, and 56:15–24 — the three citations the Raëlian canon engages directly in Extra-Terrestrials Took Me To Their Planet. The project engages Islam respectfully and technically; this is not a full-Qur'an translation and does not claim to be.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: Arabic Qur'an (Tanzil.net, Uthmanic Hafs recension).
  • Reference format: QUR-WOH-{sura}:{ayah}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 3.

Scope

Initial scope: Sura 21:1–5 (the Prophets sura — cosmological opening), Sura 54:1 (the Moon sura — the splitting of the moon), Sura 56:15–24 (the Inevitable Reality sura — the paradise verses). The Edition track (working from a Public Domain English translation with the WoH glossary applied) is available as a fallback if Translation-track Arabic competence is the project's constraint.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Revelation
entry.content:
The Apocalypse of John is the strongest WoH-interpretive-payoff text in the New Testament. The throne-vision of chapter 4 — the twenty-four elders, the four living creatures, the seven lamps — reads as a continuation of the Ezekiel-1 throne tradition. The Raëlian canon engages Revelation directly in The New Revelations, reading chapters 1, 4, 6 onward as a dictation given to John through what is described as a visual-display apparatus.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: polytonic Greek New Testament (Tregelles 1857–1879, Public Domain).
  • Reference format: REV-WOH-{chapter}:{verse}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 4.

Scope

Initial scope: complete book. The throne-vision sequence (chs 4–6), the seven seals/trumpets/bowls structure, and the new-Jerusalem material of chapters 21–22 will carry the heaviest commentary.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Shi'ur Qomah (curated selection)
entry.content:
Shi'ur Qomah ("Measurement of the Body") is the Hebrew esoteric corpus from the Hekhalot literature that the Raëlian canon implicitly engages when it cites "the Kabbalah" for the parasangs-measurement and the "height of the creator" passages (The End of the World, chapter At the Root of All Religions). This translation closes one of the genuine source-tensions named in the Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon wiki entry: the canon generically names "the Kabbalah" where the actual textual source is the Shi'ur Qomah body of literature.

About this planned translation

  • Status: Planned. Not yet drafted.
  • Source text: Hebrew (specific source edition TBD at translation start).
  • Reference format: SHQM-WOH-{section}:{line}
  • Roadmap position: Phase 5.

Scope

Initial scope: a curated selection of the parasangs passages and related cosmological material the Raëlian canon implicitly engages. Specific source edition and section selection to be determined at translation start. This is not a complete book translation — the Shi'ur Qomah/Hekhalot corpus is large, fragmentary, and would require dedicated scholarly competence the project has not yet committed to building.

How this translation will be produced

This translation will go through the standard Wheel of Heaven Translation Program pipeline: source acquisition, Translator agent (literal English applying the production glossary), Editor agent (lexical-decision adjudication, per-verse commentary, glossary deltas), Reviewer agent (independent verification with mandatory scholarly citations), and human sign-off. The full methodology is documented at Source-Text Translation.

The Wheel of Heaven lens lives in the apparatus — the glossary entries and per-verse commentary — not in the translated text itself. Accuracy above lens; where the source admits multiple defensible readings, the Raëlian canon is the tiebreaker, and every divergence from the standard translation tradition is recorded with a claim_type label and a philological rationale.

When this translation begins

See the translation roadmap for the project's current phase ordering and the conditions under which new texts move from "planned" to "in-progress."

See also

  • Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon — the catalogue of texts the Raëlian canon engages
  • Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) — the first WoH Translation in the program

entry.title: Genesis
entry.content:
Genesis (Wheel of Heaven Translation) is the first published chapter of the Wheel of Heaven Translation Program. Genesis 1 is rendered from the pointed Hebrew Masoretic text (Westminster Leningrad Codex, public domain) under a documented WoH lens, with the source and the English translation displayed in parallel.

Verses with commentary carry a bracketed marker [ ] at the end of the translated line. Click the marker to open the commentary panel. Commentaries surface the lexical decisions that distinguish this rendering from the inherited English translations — Elohim plural, the bara/asah verb pattern, raqia as a hammered-out dome, the cohortative na'aseh in v26, tselem and demut as physical resemblance, the polemical refusal of shemesh and yareach in v14–16, and others.

This is a draft. The translation is AI-drafted (Claude Opus 4.7) against translation-glossary v1.0.0, pending human review.

entry.title: Let's Welcome the Extraterrestrials
entry.content:
This page displays "Let's Welcome the Extraterrestrials" with interactive features for viewing the original French text alongside the published English translation.

The book contains 4 chapters: a wide-ranging Q&A addressing the most common questions raised after the first two messages, the new revelations Raël was permitted to share after a three-year waiting period, the framing of Raëlism as an atheist religion, and a collection of commentaries and testimonials from Raëlians.
